summary: Gruber recounts a 15-year-old story he never told on Daring Fireball: in April 2010, Steve Jobs personally recommended Gruber's analysis of Apple's controversial App Store Section 3.3.1 change (banning Flash-to-iPhone compilers) in an email to a complaining developer, calling it 'very insightful and not negative.' Gruber's original analysis argued that Apple's ban on cross-platform toolkits was ruthless but strategically logical — preventing any company from establishing a meta-platform on top of iOS. Despite Jobs's endorsement going viral at the time, Gruber never linked to it out of a misguided attempt to play it cool. He now corrects that omission, backdating a post to 2010, and reflects on what a remarkably eventful month April 2010 was for Apple and the tech industry.
key_points: In April 2010, Apple banned Flash-to-iPhone compilers via Section 3.3.1, and Gruber's analysis argued this was strategically sound to prevent cross-platform meta-platforms from undermining iOS lock-in
Steve Jobs personally emailed a developer a link to Gruber's post, calling it 'very insightful and not negative' — confirming Daring Fireball was read at the highest levels of Apple
Gruber never linked to or mentioned the Jobs endorsement on Daring Fireball for 15 years, out of what he now calls misguided modesty
Gruber's 2010 prediction that the App Store could become a 'license to print money' platform like Windows proved prescient
April 2010 was a pivotal month: iPad launch, HP buying Palm, Apple acquiring Siri, the iPhone 4 prototype leak, and Jobs publishing 'Thoughts on Flash'
money_quotes: The App Store platform could turn into a long-term de facto standard platform. That's how Microsoft became Microsoft.
That seems prescient. (The 'license to print money' part — not the 'RIM is still growing' part.)
We think John Gruber's post is very insightful and not negative.

So, what would you do if Steve Jobs was quoted in a viral blog post saying, 'We think Your Name Here's post is very insightful and not negative'? I decided to just sit there with a smug look on my face for a few days (which, arguably, isn't all that different from what I do most days).
Given that Steve Jobs was reading Daring Fireball, I didn't want him to read a post from me acting like it was a big deal that he'd recommended a piece I wrote and agreed with it.
That was pretty stupid on my part. Or at least silly. My older perspective, today, is not to overthink such things.
I think Steve Jobs's post was very insightful and not negative.
key_insight: Gruber finally tells the story of Steve Jobs endorsing his analysis of Apple's cross-platform toolkit ban, a 15-year-old omission driven by misguided modesty that he now corrects with the wisdom of hindsight.
